data.title: Mass and Time
data.name: Mass and Time
data.summary:
I’m treating this as a serious personal inquiry rather than a finished theory. The project turns a bold idea, that time may help generate or stabilize what we call mass, into three concrete modeling tracks: a feedback simulation, a minimal quantum stability model, and a relativistic conceptual visualization. The useful part is that the idea has to face equations, parameter choices, and saved outputs instead of staying at the level of language.

The audience is people who are comfortable with scientific reasoning and code, including technically curious readers, independent researchers, and physics-adjacent builders who value inspectable assumptions over polished certainty. What makes it distinct is the tone and structure: I keep the claim ambitious, then narrow it carefully, and I separate what is computed from what is interpretive. Some uncertainty is still central, especially around physical realism, conservation behavior, and how far these toy models can responsibly be taken.
data.oneLiner: A simulation-backed physics project that tests whether temporal persistence can help explain mass, orbital stability, and gravity, while staying honest about what is model output versus interpretation.

assets.label: Horizon Zoom: Extreme Gravitational Time Ratio
assets.summary: This horizon zoom focuses on the region near the black hole’s Schwarzschild radius, where gravitational time effects become more extreme. Tightening the radial window around the horizon reduced the minimum gravitational time ratio from 0.0995 to 0.0316, emphasizing how rapidly temporal behavior changes near this boundary. This image works well as a technical companion to the broader photon–black hole duality map. It gives viewers a more focused look at the extreme end of the model, where spatial confinement, horizon behavior, and temporal distortion become most visually pronounced.
assets.oneLiner: A close-up of the black-hole horizon region showing intensified gravitational time distortion.

assets.label: Photon–Black Hole Duality Map
assets.summary: This image presents the photon–black hole duality simulation, which compares horizon behavior, lightlike motion, and a broader polarity diagram in a single figure. The default configuration models a 10-solar-mass black hole, corresponding to a Schwarzschild radius of 29.538 km. Conceptually, the visualization frames photons and black holes as opposite endpoints: photon-like behavior as an extreme associated with lightlike motion, and black-hole-like behavior as an extreme associated with horizon-level spatial confinement. This is one of the most portfolio-friendly visuals because it translates a complex theoretical idea into a clear geometric spectrum.
assets.oneLiner: A polarity diagram comparing lightlike motion and black-hole horizon behavior as opposite geometric limits.

assets.label: Orbital Temporal Stability and Resonance
assets.summary: This image visualizes the orbital temporal stability experiment, which tests how a simple orbital system responds to temporal perturbation. Because QuTiP was not installed in the simulation environment, the run used a manual NumPy RK4 solver. The default perturbed run produced variance of 0.1231 with a maximum deviation of 0.6535, compared with a cleaner control run that reached a lower maximum deviation of 0.5115. The simulation sweep peaked near ω = 0.1000, suggesting a resonance region worth further investigation. In the portfolio, this image helps show that the project is not only conceptual but also tests the stability implications of temporal variation.
assets.oneLiner: A minimal orbital model testing whether temporal perturbations produce instability or resonance bands.

assets.label: High-Alpha Clock Distortion Test
assets.summary: This image shows the final state of the mass-time emergence model under a stronger alpha setting. Increasing alpha to 0.5 deepened the slowest local clock rate from 0.7955 in the baseline run to 0.0001, creating a much more extreme temporal distortion. This comparison is useful because it shows how sensitive the model is to temporal coupling strength. In the portfolio narrative, this image can serve as a “parameter stress test,” demonstrating that the simulation is not just a static visualization but a tunable model whose behavior changes substantially under different assumptions.
assets.oneLiner: A stress test showing how stronger temporal coupling dramatically slows the local clock field.

assets.label: Animated Mass-Time Emergence
assets.summary: This animation shows the time evolution of the mass-time emergence model. Instead of presenting a single final state, it reveals how the field changes step by step as mass presence interacts with a locally varying clock structure. The animation is useful as the main visual anchor for the project because it gives viewers an intuitive sense of emergence, feedback, and field evolution. It communicates the idea that mass is not merely placed into a fixed background, but may be modeled as something that changes alongside local temporal conditions.
assets.oneLiner: An animation of mass presence evolving dynamically in response to local changes in clock rate.

assets.label: Mass-Time Emergence: Baseline Field Evolution
assets.summary: This image shows the final state of a baseline mass-time simulation. The model evolves an initial mass distribution against a clock field whose local rate changes over time, allowing the simulation to test whether slowed local time can reshape mass presence and generate a gravitational response. In this run, total mass increased from 1.0000 to 1.6140 while producing a maximum gravitational potential magnitude of 0.1836. As a portfolio visual, this image represents the project’s core hypothesis: that mass and gravitational behavior can be explored as emergent effects of temporal structure rather than treated only as static spatial quantities.
assets.oneLiner: A baseline simulation showing how mass presence evolves within a locally distorted clock field.

assets.label: Simulation Overview: Time, Mass, and Geometric Extremes
assets.summary: This overview contact sheet presents the full simulation batch as a unified visual system. It brings together three experimental models that explore different aspects of the Time and Mass framework: how mass presence might evolve under locally varying clock rates, how temporal perturbations may affect orbital stability, and how photons and black holes can be interpreted as opposite geometric limits. The image functions as a high-level map of the project, giving viewers an immediate sense of the visual language, simulation outputs, and conceptual scope before they enter the individual experiments.
assets.oneLiner: A visual summary of the project’s three simulation tracks: mass-time emergence, temporal orbital stability, and photon–black hole duality.

readme.content:
# Time and Mass: A Time-Centric Ontology of Mass, Gravity, and Spacetime

> *Space is where mass is. Time is why mass is. Gravity is how mass is felt.*

## Overview

This project explores a speculative but structured physics hypothesis: **time is the fundamental substrate from which mass emerges**. Mass is not a static property but a persistent resonance of quantum-field excitations sustained through continuous temporal evolution. Gravity, in turn, is the wave-like propagation of that persistence through spacetime.

The hypothesis inverts the standard GR causal arrow: instead of "mass warps time," it proposes "time generates mass."

## Core Ideas (7 Pillars)

| # | Pillar | Key Claim |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 1 | [Time Generates Mass](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/01_time_generates_mass.md) | Mass emerges from persistent quantum excitations evolving through time |
| 2 | [Mass Flux Density](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/02_mass_flux_density.md) | (kg/s)/m captures gravitational character better than static mass |
| 3 | [Relativistic Mass Distribution](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/03_relativistic_mass_distribution.md) | At relativistic speeds, mass becomes spatially non-localized |
| 4 | [Gravity = Wave, Mass = Particle](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/04_gravity_wave_mass_particle.md) | Wave-particle duality extends to gravity-mass relationship |
| 5 | [Temporal Resonance Orbitals](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/05_temporal_resonance_orbitals.md) | Electron orbitals are stable because temporal interference averages to zero |
| 6 | [Photon–Black Hole Duality](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/06_photon_blackhole_duality.md) | Photons (pure time) and black holes (pure space) are extremes of a polarity spectrum |
| 7 | [Strong Force as Temporal Gradient](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/07_strong_force_temporal_gradient.md) | Nuclear binding from short-range temporal coherence equilibration |

Full synthesis: [theory/00_core_hypothesis.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/theory/00_core_hypothesis.md)

## Simulations

Three numerical simulations test aspects of this hypothesis. Each has adjustable parameters via YAML config files, a Python CLI script, and a Jupyter notebook.

### Quick Start

```bash
cd simulations
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Run any simulation:
python mass_time_emergence/simulation.py
python orbital_temporal_stability/simulation.py
python photon_blackhole_duality/visualization.py

# Or generate a full headless batch with saved artifacts:
python run_batch.py

# Or use Jupyter notebooks for interactive exploration:
jupyter notebook
```

### Available Simulations

| Simulation | Tests Pillars | Description |
|---|---|---|
| [Mass-Time Emergence](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/simulations/mass_time_emergence/) | 1, 2 | 1D model of mass density evolving from time flow with gravitational coupling |
| [Orbital Temporal Stability](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/simulations/orbital_temporal_stability/) | 5 | QuTiP two-level atom: does temporal perturbation destabilize orbitals? |
| [Photon–BH Duality](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/simulations/photon_blackhole_duality/) | 6 | Schwarzschild metric visualization showing space-time polarity spectrum |

All parameters are adjustable in each simulation's `config.yaml`.

## Directory Structure

```
Time and Mass/
├── README.md                    ← You are here
├── PLAN.md                      ← Implementation plan (phases, status)
├── theory/                      ← 8 curated theory documents
├── simulations/
│   ├── requirements.txt
│   ├── shared/config_loader.py  ← Shared YAML config utility
│   ├── mass_time_emergence/     ← Simulation 1
│   ├── orbital_temporal_stability/  ← Simulation 2
│   └── photon_blackhole_duality/    ← Simulation 3
├── analysis/                    ← Future: real data pipelines
├── notes/
│   ├── journal/                 ← Dated entries (2025_07_01, etc.)
│   └── thought_experiments/     ← Idea sketches (rain analogy, gravity, etc.)
├── references/
│   ├── chatgpt_discussions/     ← AI-assisted exploration transcripts
│   ├── literature_to_review.md  ← Books, papers, datasets to study
│   ├── proposed_experiments.md  ← Testable experiments (at-home + funded)
│   └── Mass in a timeless universe.pdf
├── images/                      ← Diagrams and screenshots
└── archive/                     ← Original files (preserved verbatim)
```

## Future Work (Phase 4+)

- Relativistic mass delocalization simulation
- 2D gravity wave propagation
- CERN Open Data analysis (muon momentum distributions)
- Atomic clock / GPS data correlation
- LIGO gravitational wave analysis
- Machine learning pattern detection

See [PLAN.md](https://github.com/ZSturman/Time-and-Mass/blob/main/PLAN.md) for the full roadmap.
